_meta.description: Wave 5 maintenance, EHS, compliance, and lean-governance policy for practical lifecycle closure.

objective.name: Maintenance, EHS, And Compliance Closure
objective.statement: Every maintenance, permit, incident, safety, and 5S object must exist for a concrete plant-control purpose, run on a practical lifecycle instead of placeholder status aliases, and close through one auditable backend owner with lean follow-up and gate control.

build_questions.question: What factory gate, uptime risk, permit-to-work constraint, safety exposure, or lean-discipline loop is this object born to control, and what real plant failure appears if it does not exist?
Do the lifecycle states describe real operating decisions such as approve, activate, contain, suspend, verify, retire, or archive, instead of placeholder digital-thread statuses?
Does the canonical resource point to the live table that production actually uses, or is it still hiding behind a legacy alias or non-existent placeholder table?
If a maintenance console or register is only a contextual alias, can it trace back to the canonical master identity without creating a second state truth?
Which server-side roles may approve, activate, suspend, close, retire, or archive the object, and are those guards enforced on the backend transitions?
Can an auditor trace from equipment, PM plan, PM work order, permit, incident, observation, or 5S audit through one canonical chain with timeline, status, and closure evidence?
Does the object feed maintenance review, permit review, incident review, safety review, or gemba follow-up so the backend supports continuous improvement instead of dead records?
If the element has no unique operational purpose, has it been isolated as alias, archive, or unused instead of left live by habit?
